sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Hypertension (high blood pressure) is defined as a persistent elevation of blood pressure (BP) in the arteries and is typically asymptomatic (“silent killer”). Hypertension can be primary (essential [90-95% of cases]) where it has no single identifiable cause and may develop due to genetic and lifestyle factors, or secondary which is caused by an underlying medical condition.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Headache, (especially occipital in the mornings)
- Visual disturbances, dizziness
- Chest pain, palpitations
- Shortness of breath
- Peripheral oedema
- Epistaxis (nose bleeds)
- ECG changes (hypertrophy, LBBB, etc)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Some patients’ BP may be elevated in certain medical situations (“white coat”)
- Allow the patient to rest, seated or semi-recumbent for example, for 5 minutes before taking their BP
- Consider taking a manual BP first as a baseline
- Ensure medics are using the correct size BP cuff for the patient
- There is NO place for a systolic only BP measurement in this context
- Patients with severely elevated BP associated with life-threatening complications, myocardial or cerebral ischaemia for example, must be treated urgently
- Severely elevated BP (180/100 mmHg) without symptoms can be treated semi-urgently within days
- Hypertensive urgency, BP ≥ 180/110 mmHg with symptoms (headache/dizziness), should be treated within hours
- Hypertensive emergency, BP ≥ 220/140 mmHg, with or without symptoms, require urgent treatment
- Rapid reduction of a severely elevated BP or hypertensive urgency may cause more harm than good

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Take multiple BP readings, several minutes apart
- Limb LEAD ECG
- Consider Secondary Survey
- Consider urinalysis (specifically protein)
- Consider pregnancy test if females
- Consider GTN in hypertensive emergency in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- 12 LEAD ECG
- Consider anti-hypertensive medication depending on cause in consultation with on- call doctor
- Amlodipine, Metoprolol, Frusemide
- Consider IV metoprolol in hypertensive emergency in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider analgesia if hypertensive secondary to pain

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Reference Ranges
- Normal: <120/80 mmHg
- Elevated: 120–129/<80 mmHg
- Stage 1 Hypertension: 130–139/80–89 mmHg
- Stage 2 Hypertension: ≥140/90 mmHg
- If available, patients can take a blood pressure machine away and record their own BP morning, noon and night for several days for a more accurate estimation of their BP 19 RESPIRATORY
